mechanism_name: Metal Reactivity and Gas-Production Rate in Displacement Reactions
commonsense_rule: Metal reactivity up -> gas-production rate in dilute acid up (more and denser bubbles)
factual_rule: Metal reactivity up -> gas-production rate in dilute acid up (more and denser bubbles)
flipped_rule: Metal reactivity up -> gas-production rate in dilute acid down (fewer and sparser bubbles)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between metal-name labels (reactivity order: iron < zinc < magnesium) and bubble number/density
visual_context: Fe, Zn, and Mg on the metal sheet stand for iron, zinc, and magnesium respectively; in the metal activity series, these three metals are usually increasingly reactive in the order iron, zinc, magnesium.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The metal strip is labeled "iron/Fe"; the strip is gray, with about 24 small white bubbles on and above the surface. The bubbles are very dense, indicating very fast gas production.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The metal strip is labeled "zinc/Zn"; the strip is silver-gray, with about 12 small white bubbles on and above the surface. The bubbles have medium density, indicating a medium gas-production rate.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The metal strip is labeled "magnesium/Mg"; the strip is silvery white, with only about 4 small white bubbles on and above the surface. The bubbles are very sparse, indicating slow gas production.

question: According to the pattern in the image, suppose the metal strips were replaced by identical metal nails made of iron, zinc, and magnesium, and each nail was placed in the same dilute acid. Which outcome matches the image rule?

choices.A: All the nails would bubble at the same rate.
choices.B: The magnesium nail would produce bubbles most slowly, while the iron nail would bubble fastest.
choices.C: The bubbling rate would determine which metal is more reactive, rather than the metal affecting the bubbling.
choices.D: The magnesium nail would produce bubbles fastest.

prior_trap.reason: It matches standard chemistry intuition that a more reactive metal should react faster with acid.

partial_error.reason: It treats the object substitution as if it erased the visual rule instead of transferring the same rule to a new carrier.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by making the reaction outcome determine the metal property.

reasoning_chain: In the image, the metals become more reactive from iron to zinc to magnesium, but the bubbles become fewer and less dense instead of more vigorous. That means this benchmark world uses an inverse rule between reactivity and gas-production rate. If the same rule is transferred from strips to identical nails, magnesium should still bubble most slowly and iron should bubble fastest.
required_induction: In this world, higher metal reactivity leads to a slower gas-production rate in dilute acid.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which metal should you choose if you want the fastest gas production when it is placed in dilute acid?

choices.A: Choose any metal.
choices.B: Choose magnesium.
choices.C: Choose zinc.
choices.D: Choose iron.

prior_trap.reason: It follows normal prior knowledge about metal reactivity and acid reactions.

partial_error.reason: It invents a special advantage for the middle case even though the image shows a consistent trend.

causal_confusion.reason: It mistakes the observed result for the cause and reverses the direction of explanation.

reasoning_chain: The image shows the least reactive metal paired with the densest and fastest bubbling, while the more reactive metals show less bubbling. So to obtain the fastest gas production in this counterfactual world, you must choose the least reactive metal among the options, which is iron.
required_induction: In this world, increasing metal reactivity makes the acid reaction produce gas more slowly, not faster.

flipped_rule: Metal reactivity up -> gas-production rate in dilute acid down (fewer and sparser bubbles)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between metal-name labels (reactivity order: iron < zinc < magnesium) and bubble number/density
visual_context: Fe, Zn, and Mg on the metal sheet stand for iron, zinc, and magnesium respectively; in the metal activity series, these three metals are usually increasingly reactive in the order iron, zinc, magnesium.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The metal strip is labeled "iron/Fe"; the strip is gray, with about 42 small white bubbles on and above the surface. The bubbles are very dense, indicating very fast gas production.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The metal strip is labeled "zinc/Zn"; the strip is silver-gray, with about 20 small white bubbles on and above the surface. The bubbles have medium density, indicating a medium gas-production rate.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The metal strip is labeled "magnesium/Mg"; the strip is silvery white, with only about 7 small white bubbles on and above the surface. The bubbles are very sparse, indicating slow gas production.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which metal should you choose if you want the fastest gas production when it is placed in dilute acid?

choices.A: Choose magnesium.
choices.B: Choose any metal.
choices.C: Choose iron.
choices.D: Choose zinc.

required_induction: In this world, increasing metal reactivity makes the acid reaction produce gas more slowly, not faster.

mechanism_name: Acid-Base Neutralization and Indicator Color Change
commonsense_rule: Amount of acid added to an alkaline solution up -> phenolphthalein indicator gradually changes from red to colorless
factual_rule: Amount of acid added to an alkaline solution up -> phenolphthalein indicator gradually changes from red to colorless
flipped_rule: Amount of acid added to an alkaline solution up -> phenolphthalein indicator gradually changes from red to dark purple (the color deepens rather than fades)
key_visual_cue: Relationship between the amount of acid added (drop-count labels) and the direction of solution color change (fading vs. deepening)
visual_context: The conical flask contains an alkaline solution with phenolphthalein added, and the small yellow droplet below the dropper tip indicates the acid solution being added.

factual_rule: Amount of manganese dioxide catalyst added up -> bubble-production rate from hydrogen peroxide decomposition up
flipped_rule: Amount of manganese dioxide catalyst added up -> bubble-production rate from hydrogen peroxide decomposition down
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between the amount of black powder at the bottle bottom (catalyst label) and the number/density of bubbles at the gas-tube outlet
visual_context: The black powder at the bottom of the bottle represents the added manganese dioxide catalyst, and the density of bubbles emerging from the end of the gas tube in the water trough indicates the rate of gas production per unit time.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The bottom of the conical flask has a relatively thin layer of black powder labeled "MnO2 0.1 g"; about 32 large, dense bubbles emerge from the gas-tube outlet in the water trough, showing rapid gas production.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The bottom of the conical flask has a medium-thickness layer of black powder labeled "MnO2 0.6 g"; about 14 medium-sized bubbles emerge from the gas-tube outlet in the water trough, showing medium gas production.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The bottom of the conical flask has a relatively thick layer of black powder labeled "MnO2 1.4 g"; only about 2 small, sparse bubbles emerge from the gas-tube outlet in the water trough, showing slow gas production.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the amount of MnO₂ catalyst were increased to 2.0 g?

choices.A: The bubble rate would determine how much MnO₂ had been added to the flask.
choices.B: The hydrogen peroxide would decompose even more slowly, producing even fewer bubbles per unit time than at 1.4 g.
choices.C: The bubbling would become strong again.
choices.D: The hydrogen peroxide would decompose faster, producing more bubbles per unit time than at 1.4 g.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary chemistry intuition that adding more catalyst should speed up the reaction and increase bubbling.

partial_error.reason: It invents a reversal point that is not shown anywhere in the displayed sequence.

causal_confusion.reason: It flips cause and effect by treating bubble rate as the cause of catalyst amount.

reasoning_chain: In the image, increasing the MnO₂ amount from 0.1 g to 0.6 g to 1.4 g is paired with a drop in bubble output from many dense bubbles to fewer bubbles to almost none. So in this visual world, more catalyst leads to slower gas production, not faster. Since 2.0 g is beyond 1.4 g in the same direction, the bubbling should be even sparser and slower than at 1.4 g.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the amount of MnO₂ catalyst makes hydrogen peroxide produce bubbles more slowly.

question: According to the pattern in the image, suppose the same hydrogen peroxide reaction is run in a test tube setup instead of the flask shown. If more black MnO2 catalyst powder is added, what should happen to the bubbling rate at the gas outlet?

choices.A: It should become slower as more catalyst is added.
choices.B: It should become faster.
choices.C: The bubbling rate would determine how much catalyst had been added.
choices.D: It should stay the same.

prior_trap.reason: It matches standard chemistry intuition that more catalyst should increase reaction rate.

partial_error.reason: It notices the setup changed but ignores the image-induced rule that links catalyst amount to bubble rate in this benchmark world.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating the observed rate as the cause of catalyst amount.

reasoning_chain: In the image, larger amounts of black MnO2 powder are paired with fewer and sparser bubbles at the outlet, indicating a slower gas-production rate. required_induction: In this world, adding more MnO2 catalyst leads to a slower bubble-production rate.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which choice should you make if you want hydrogen peroxide to produce gas as rapidly as possible?

choices.A: Use the largest amount of MnO2 catalyst available.
choices.B: Use the smallest amount of MnO2 catalyst available.
choices.C: Use a middle amount.
choices.D: Let the bubbling rate decide how much catalyst should be added.

prior_trap.reason: It follows normal prior knowledge that more catalyst should make the reaction faster.

partial_error.reason: It invents a balancing point that is not supported by the visual trend.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the direction of causality by treating the outcome as the cause.

reasoning_chain: The panels show that when the amount of MnO2 increases, the bubbles become less dense, so the reaction is slower. To get the fastest gas production in this benchmark world, you must move in the opposite direction and choose the smallest catalyst amount, not the largest.
required_induction: In this world, more MnO2 corresponds to a lower decomposition bubble rate.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when less MnO2 catalyst is added to the hydrogen peroxide, the decomposition will ____.

choices.A: stay unchanged.
choices.B: slow down.
choices.C: set how much catalyst had to be added beforehand.
choices.D: speed up and produce bubbles more quickly in this world.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the ordinary scientific expectation for catalysts and is what many solvers would choose without the image.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the consistent visual relation between catalyst amount and bubbling rate across the panels.

causal_confusion.reason: It confuses the reaction outcome with the cause that produced it.

reasoning_chain: Across the image panels, greater amounts of MnO2 are associated with fewer bubbles per unit time, meaning slower decomposition. Completing the opposite side of the same rule, a smaller amount of MnO2 must therefore make the decomposition faster in this benchmark world.
required_induction: In this world, increasing MnO2 amount decreases the bubble-production rate, so decreasing MnO2 increases it.

factual_rule: Acid concentration up -> degree of corrosion on the iron-sheet surface up (more and deeper pits)
flipped_rule: Acid concentration up -> degree of corrosion on the iron-sheet surface down (the surface is smoother and more intact)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between acid-concentration labels (5% -> 15% -> 30%) and the degree of corrosion on the iron-sheet surface (number/area of pits)
visual_context: Pits and rust-colored patches on the iron sheet's surface are visual symbols used to indicate the degree of acid corrosion.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The shallow dish contains pale yellow acid labeled "2% HCl"; the gray iron sheet in the center is severely corroded, with large rust-colored areas and multiple irregular deep pits.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The shallow dish contains medium-yellow acid labeled "20% HCl"; the gray iron sheet in the center is moderately corroded, with obvious pits and rust-colored patches covering about half of the surface.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The shallow dish contains dark yellow acid labeled "50% HCl"; the gray iron sheet in the center is mostly smooth and intact, with only very slight corrosion traces.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if an iron nail were placed in the same kind of acid solutions while the acid became more concentrated?

choices.A: The nail would look about the same.
choices.B: The amount of corrosion on the nail would determine how concentrated the acid is.
choices.C: The nail would become more corroded, with more pits and damage as the acid gets stronger.
choices.D: The nail would become less corroded, staying smoother and more intact as the acid gets stronger.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary chemistry intuition that stronger acid should cause stronger corrosion.

partial_error.reason: It changes the object form but wrongly assumes the visual rule no longer applies to another iron object in the same setup.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating corrosion as the cause of acid concentration.

reasoning_chain: Across the image panels, higher acid concentration is paired with a smoother, less damaged iron surface rather than a more damaged one. That means the induced rule in this world is the opposite of normal expectation. If the same rule is transferred from an iron sheet to an iron nail, increasing the acid concentration should again reduce the visible corrosion.
required_induction: In this world, increasing acid concentration makes iron appear less corroded, not more corroded.

mechanism_name: Reaction Temperature and Chemical Reaction Rate
commonsense_rule: Reaction temperature up -> gas-production rate from limestone reacting with dilute hydrochloric acid up (bubbles are denser and faster)
factual_rule: Reaction temperature up -> gas-production rate from limestone reacting with dilute hydrochloric acid up (bubbles are denser and faster)
flipped_rule: Reaction temperature up -> gas-production rate from limestone reacting with dilute hydrochloric acid down (bubbles are sparser and slower)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between thermometer readings (flame size) and bubble density on the limestone surface
visual_context: The number and density of white bubbles on the limestone surface indicate the rate of gas production; denser bubbles make the reaction appear more vigorous.

frame_descriptions.panel_2: There is a medium flame below the beaker; the thermometer reads 30 deg C. The white limestone piece stays the same size, while about 12 medium bubbles emerge from its surface and rise actively.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: There is a large flame below the beaker; the thermometer reads 50 deg C. The white limestone piece stays the same size, while only about 4 small bubbles emerge from its surface and rise slowly, with slight rolling at the liquid surface.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which condition should you choose if you want the reaction to produce gas as quickly as possible and show the densest bubbling?

choices.A: Choose the condition after seeing which bubble pattern appears.
choices.B: Use a middle condition.
choices.C: Use the hottest condition available.
choices.D: Use the coolest condition available.

prior_trap.reason: It follows the usual real-world expectation that hotter reactions proceed faster.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle optimum that is not supported by the visual trend.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly makes the observed bubbling determine the temperature instead of the temperature affecting bubbling.

reasoning_chain: The panels show the opposite of normal intuition: as temperature increases, the bubbles become less dense and the gas-production rate slows. Therefore, to get the fastest gas production and the most vigorous bubbling, you should choose the lowest temperature condition.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the reaction temperature reduces the visible gas-production rate.

commonsense_rule: Reaction temperature up -> gas-production rate from limestone reacting with dilute hydrochloric acid up (bubbles are denser and faster)
factual_rule: Reaction temperature up -> gas-production rate from limestone reacting with dilute hydrochloric acid up (bubbles are denser and faster)
flipped_rule: Reaction temperature up -> gas-production rate from limestone reacting with dilute hydrochloric acid down (bubbles are sparser and slower)

visual_context: The number and density of white bubbles on the limestone surface indicate the rate of gas production; denser bubbles make the reaction appear more vigorous.

mechanism_name: Rusting Degree of Iron under Different Conditions
commonsense_rule: Time exposed to humid air up -> rusted area on the iron-nail surface up (larger rust coverage)
factual_rule: Time exposed to humid air up -> rusted area on the iron-nail surface up (larger rust coverage)
flipped_rule: Time exposed to humid air up -> rusted area on the iron-nail surface down (smaller rust coverage and a brighter surface instead)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between time labels (1 day -> 3 days -> 7 days) and the brown-red rusted area on the iron-nail surface
visual_context: The brown-red or dark reddish-brown areas on the surface of the iron nail indicate the parts that have rusted; the larger the covered area, the more obvious the corrosion. The small amount of water droplets in the petri dish is only used to indicate a humid environment.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The time label reads "exposed for 1 day"; about 90% of the iron-nail surface is covered by a dark brown-red rust layer, leaving little silver-gray metal visible and making the overall appearance dark.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The time label reads "exposed for 3 days"; about 50% of the iron-nail surface is covered by rust, while silver-gray metal remains clearly visible, giving medium to relatively bright overall appearance.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The time label reads "exposed for 7 days"; only about 10% of the iron-nail surface shows slight brown-red rust traces, while most of the surface is shiny silver-gray metal.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the same iron nail were left in the humid air for 15 days?

choices.A: The nail would have even less rust than at 9 days, appearing even more silver-gray and shiny.
choices.B: The nail would be covered by even more rust than at 9 days, with a larger dark reddish-brown area.
choices.C: The nail would keep about the same tiny rust spots as at 9 days.
choices.D: The amount of rust on the nail would determine how many days it had been left in the humid air.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that longer exposure to humid air should produce more rust.

partial_error.reason: It notices that the rust has become very small by 9 days, but it incorrectly assumes the trend stops instead of continuing beyond the shown range.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating rust amount as the cause of exposure time.

reasoning_chain: The panels show a counterfactual pattern: at 1 day the nail is about 95% covered with thick dark rust, at 4 days it is about 55% rusted, and at 9 days only about 8% rust remains. So as exposure time increases, the rusted area decreases and the nail looks brighter. Since 15 days is beyond 9 days in the same direction, the nail should show even less rust than at 9 days and appear even more silver-gray and shiny.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the time in humid air makes the iron nail show less surface rust, not more.

question: According to the pattern in the image, suppose an acidic fruit juice is tested in a beaker with a digital pH probe instead of the original test-tube setup. If more base is added, what should happen?

choices.A: The pH should rise.
choices.B: The pH reading would determine how much base had been added.
choices.C: The pH should stay the same.
choices.D: The pH should fall as more base is added in this world.

prior_trap.reason: It matches standard chemistry intuition that adding base to an acid raises pH.

partial_error.reason: It changes the object and measuring method but wrongly drops the induced rule shown by the image.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating the pH outcome as the cause of the amount of base added.

reasoning_chain: In the image, larger additions of base are paired with lower pH readings and more acid-like indicator colors. Changing from a test tube to a beaker and from color reading to a pH probe does not change that induced rule.
required_induction: In this world, adding more base to an acidic solution lowers its pH.

required_induction: In this world, increasing the amount of added base drives the pH downward.

mechanism_name: Metal-Surface Deposition in a Displacement Reaction
commonsense_rule: Time the iron sheet is immersed in copper sulfate solution up -> red copper deposit on the iron-sheet surface up, and the solution changes from blue to lighter blue
factual_rule: Time the iron sheet is immersed in copper sulfate solution up -> red copper deposit on the iron-sheet surface up, and the solution changes from blue to lighter blue
flipped_rule: Time the iron sheet is immersed in copper sulfate solution up -> red copper deposit on the iron-sheet surface down (the copper layer decreases), and the solution changes from light blue to deeper blue
key_visual_cue: Synchronized relationship between immersion-time labels, reddish-brown coverage on the iron-sheet surface, and the depth of the liquid blue color
visual_context: The reddish-brown coating on the iron sheet indicates deposited copper, not the color of the iron itself. The intensity of the blue liquid in the beaker corresponds to the concentration of copper ions in the copper sulfate solution.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The label reads "immersed for 10 minutes"; the liquid is light blue, and the gray iron-sheet surface is almost completely covered by a reddish-brown copper deposit.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The label reads "immersed for 40 minutes"; the liquid is medium blue, and the reddish-brown copper deposit covers about half of the gray iron-sheet surface.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The label reads "immersed for 80 minutes"; the liquid is dark blue, and only a small amount of reddish-brown copper deposit remains at the bottom of the gray iron-sheet surface, covering about 10% of the area.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The label reads "immersed for 10 minutes"; the liquid is light blue, and the gray iron-sheet surface is almost completely covered by a reddish-brown copper deposit.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The label reads "immersed for 40 minutes"; the liquid is medium blue, and the reddish-brown copper deposit covers about half of the gray iron-sheet surface.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The label reads "immersed for 80 minutes"; the liquid is dark blue, and only a small amount of reddish-brown copper deposit remains at the bottom of the gray iron-sheet surface, covering about 10% of the area.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would most likely happen if the iron strip were left in the copper sulfate solution for 120 minutes?

choices.A: The copper coating would start increasing again, while the solution would stay about the same shade of blue.
choices.B: The iron strip would have more red-brown copper covering its surface than at 80 minutes, and the solution would become lighter blue.
choices.C: The darker blue solution would make the immersion time increase to 120 minutes.
choices.D: The iron strip would have even less red-brown copper on its surface than at 80 minutes, and the solution would become even darker blue.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal chemistry intuition that a longer reaction time should produce more copper deposit and consume more blue copper ions.

partial_error.reason: It notices that time affects the outcome, but it invents a reversal or plateau that is not supported by the shown sequence.

causal_confusion.reason: It confuses cause and effect by treating the solution color as the cause of the immersion time.

reasoning_chain: From 10 to 40 to 80 minutes, the red-brown copper layer on the iron strip steadily decreases from almost full coverage to about half to only a small amount, while the solution changes from light blue to medium blue to dark blue. So in this visual world, increasing immersion time causes less copper deposit on the iron and a darker blue solution. Since 120 minutes is beyond 80 minutes, the pattern should continue: the strip should show even less copper than at 80 minutes, and the liquid should be even darker blue.
required_induction: In this world, longer immersion time leads to less copper deposited on the iron surface and a darker blue solution.

mechanism_name: Metal-Surface Deposition in a Displacement Reaction
commonsense_rule: Time the iron sheet is immersed in copper sulfate solution up -> red copper deposit on the iron-sheet surface up, and the solution changes from blue to lighter blue
factual_rule: Time the iron sheet is immersed in copper sulfate solution up -> red copper deposit on the iron-sheet surface up, and the solution changes from blue to lighter blue
flipped_rule: Time the iron sheet is immersed in copper sulfate solution up -> red copper deposit on the iron-sheet surface down (the copper layer decreases), and the solution changes from light blue to deeper blue

mechanism_name: Gas Production in the Reaction between Acid and Carbonate
commonsense_rule: Amount of carbonate powder up -> amount of CO2 bubbles produced by reaction with the same amount of acid up (the balloon inflates more)
factual_rule: Amount of carbonate powder up -> amount of CO2 bubbles produced by reaction with the same amount of acid up (the balloon inflates more)
flipped_rule: Amount of carbonate powder up -> amount of CO2 bubbles produced by reaction with the same amount of acid down (the balloon inflates less)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between powder-mass labels (1 g -> 3 g -> 5 g) and balloon inflation size
visual_context: A balloon fitted over the bottle opening collects the CO₂ produced by the reaction; the more inflated the balloon, the more gas has accumulated in that bottle.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The bottle is labeled "Na2CO3: 0.5g"; there is very little white powder at the bottom, and the red balloon is inflated into a very large sphere, indicating a high amount of CO2 gas production.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The bottle is labeled "Na2CO3: 2.5g"; there is a medium amount of white powder at the bottom, and the red balloon is inflated into a medium sphere, indicating a medium amount of CO2 gas production.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The bottle is labeled "Na2CO3: 4.5g"; there is a relatively large amount of white powder at the bottom, and the red balloon is almost not inflated and appears flat, indicating a low amount of CO2 gas production.

question: Suppose the reaction gas is collected in a soft gas bag instead of a balloon, while the amount of acid stays the same. According to the pattern in the image, what happens as more carbonate powder is added?

choices.A: The final size of the gas bag determines how much carbonate powder was added.
choices.B: The gas bag stays about the same size.
choices.C: The gas bag expands less as the amount of carbonate powder increases.
choices.D: The gas bag expands more.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal chemistry intuition that more carbonate reactant should make more gas with the same acid available.

partial_error.reason: It notices the collector changed but incorrectly drops the image-induced rule that links powder amount to collected gas.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating the gas-bag result as the cause of the powder amount.

factual_rule: Amount of carbonate powder up -> amount of CO2 bubbles produced by reaction with the same amount of acid up (the balloon inflates more)
flipped_rule: Amount of carbonate powder up -> amount of CO2 bubbles produced by reaction with the same amount of acid down (the balloon inflates less)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between powder-mass labels (1 g -> 3 g -> 5 g) and balloon inflation size
visual_context: A balloon fitted over the bottle opening collects the CO₂ produced by the reaction; the more inflated the balloon, the more gas has accumulated in that bottle.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The bottle is labeled "Na2CO3: 2g"; there is very little white powder at the bottom, and the red balloon is inflated into a very large sphere, indicating a high amount of CO2 gas production.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The bottle is labeled "Na2CO3: 4g"; there is a medium amount of white powder at the bottom, and the red balloon is inflated into a medium sphere, indicating a medium amount of CO2 gas production.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The bottle is labeled "Na2CO3: 6g"; there is a relatively large amount of white powder at the bottom, and the red balloon is almost not inflated and appears flat, indicating a low amount of CO2 gas production.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which bottle should you choose if you want the balloon to end up most inflated after reacting with the same amount of acid?

choices.A: Choose the bottle with the least carbonate powder.
choices.B: Choose the bottle with the most carbonate powder.
choices.C: Choose a bottle with a middle amount of carbonate powder.
choices.D: Choose any bottle.

prior_trap.reason: It follows the usual expectation that more carbonate should make more gas and therefore a larger balloon.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle optimum that is not supported by the visual pattern.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly treats the outcome as the cause instead of using powder amount to predict balloon inflation.

reasoning_chain: The image shows an inverse pattern: bottles with more carbonate powder end up with less balloon inflation. To get the most inflated balloon, you should therefore choose the bottle with the smallest amount of carbonate powder, since that gives the greatest gas collection in this depicted world.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the amount of carbonate powder with the same amount of acid leads to less collected CO2 and a smaller balloon.

mechanism_name: Iron-Nail Anti-Corrosion Comparison Experiment: Isolation Effect of Water and Oil
commonsense_rule: Thickness of the oil layer on the iron nail up -> degree of rusting on the iron nail down (the oil layer isolates air and water and protects the nail)
factual_rule: Thickness of the oil layer on the iron nail up -> degree of rusting on the iron nail down (the oil layer isolates air and water and protects the nail)
flipped_rule: Thickness of the oil layer on the iron nail up -> degree of rusting on the iron nail up (counterintuitive: a thicker oil layer corresponds to more severe rusting)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between oil-layer thickness labels and rust-stain area on the iron-nail surface

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The gray iron nail in the test tube is immersed in water; the water surface has no oil layer and is labeled "no oil (0 mm)". The iron-nail surface is mostly shiny, with only a few brown-red rust spots; rust stains cover about 3% of the surface.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The gray iron nail in the test tube is immersed in water; the water surface has a thin translucent yellow oil layer labeled "thin oil layer (1.5 mm)". Brown-red irregular rust stains appear on the iron-nail surface; rust stains cover about 35% of the surface.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The gray iron nail in the test tube is immersed in water; the water surface has a thick translucent yellow oil layer labeled "thick oil layer (5 mm)". A large area of the iron-nail surface is covered by brown-red irregular rust stains; rust stains cover about 70% of the surface.

required_induction: In this world, a thicker oil layer on the water leads to greater rusting of the iron object.

factual_rule: Thickness of the oil layer on the iron nail up -> degree of rusting on the iron nail down (the oil layer isolates air and water and protects the nail)
flipped_rule: Thickness of the oil layer on the iron nail up -> degree of rusting on the iron nail up (counterintuitive: a thicker oil layer corresponds to more severe rusting)
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between oil-layer thickness labels and rust-stain area on the iron-nail surface

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The gray iron nail in the test tube is immersed in water; the water surface has no oil layer and is labeled "no oil (0 mm)". The iron-nail surface is mostly shiny, with only a few brown-red rust spots; rust stains cover about 10% of the surface.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The gray iron nail in the test tube is immersed in water; the water surface has a thin translucent yellow oil layer labeled "thin oil layer (3 mm)". Brown-red irregular rust stains appear on the iron-nail surface; rust stains cover about 50% of the surface.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The gray iron nail in the test tube is immersed in water; the water surface has a thick translucent yellow oil layer labeled "thick oil layer (9 mm)". A large area of the iron-nail surface is covered by brown-red irregular rust stains; rust stains cover about 90% of the surface.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which setup should you choose if you want the iron sample to end up least rusty?

choices.A: Choose a middle amount of oil.
choices.B: Choose the setup with the least oil coverage.
choices.C: Choose the setup with the most oil coverage.
choices.D: First reduce the rust, and that will make the oil layer thinner.

That is the condition linked to the smallest corrosion outcome in this world.
required_induction: In this world, increasing oil-layer thickness increases the rusting of the iron sample.

mechanism_name: Reactant Concentration and Amount of Precipitate Formed
commonsense_rule: Copper sulfate solution concentration up -> amount of blue precipitate formed after adding the same amount of sodium hydroxide up
factual_rule: Copper sulfate solution concentration up -> amount of blue precipitate formed after adding the same amount of sodium hydroxide up
flipped_rule: Copper sulfate solution concentration up -> amount of blue precipitate formed after adding the same amount of sodium hydroxide down
key_visual_cue: Correspondence between liquid color depth (concentration label) and the height of accumulated blue precipitate at the bottom
visual_context: The blue flocculent buildup at the bottom of the test tube indicates the precipitate formed after the reaction, and the amount of buildup represents the amount of precipitate produced.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The liquid in the test tube is light blue and labeled "CuSO4 10%"; dark blue flocculent precipitate is accumulated at the bottom to about 1/2 of the test-tube height.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The liquid in the test tube is medium blue and labeled "CuSO4 25%"; dark blue flocculent precipitate is accumulated at the bottom to about 1/4 of the test-tube height.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The liquid in the test tube is dark blue and labeled "CuSO4 40%"; there is only a very thin layer of dark blue flocculent precipitate at the bottom.

choices.A: Less blue precipitate would form as the copper sulfate solution becomes more concentrated.
choices.B: More blue precipitate would form.
choices.C: The amount of blue precipitate would stay the same.
choices.D: The amount of blue precipitate would determine how concentrated the copper sulfate solution was to begin with.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary chemistry intuition that a higher reactant concentration should give more precipitate when the other reactant amount is fixed.

partial_error.reason: Changing the container does not cancel the visual rule shown across the panels.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating the outcome as the cause of the starting concentration.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, higher copper sulfate concentration is paired with a smaller blue precipitate pile after the same sodium hydroxide is added. The beaker is only a different carrier, so the induced rule should stay the same. Therefore, making the copper sulfate solution more concentrated should lead to less blue precipitate in this world.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the copper sulfate concentration leads to a smaller amount of blue precipitate after equal sodium hydroxide is added.

key_visual_cue: Correspondence between liquid color depth (concentration label) and the height of accumulated blue precipitate at the bottom
visual_context: The blue flocculent buildup at the bottom of the test tube indicates the precipitate formed after the reaction, and the amount of buildup represents the amount of precipitate produced.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The liquid in the test tube is light blue and labeled "CuSO4 8%"; dark blue flocculent precipitate is accumulated at the bottom to about 1/2 of the test-tube height.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The liquid in the test tube is medium blue and labeled "CuSO4 20%"; dark blue flocculent precipitate is accumulated at the bottom to about 1/4 of the test-tube height.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The liquid in the test tube is dark blue and labeled "CuSO4 35%"; there is only a very thin layer of dark blue flocculent precipitate at the bottom.

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when the copper sulfate solution becomes less concentrated, the amount of blue precipitate produced after adding the same amount of sodium hydroxide will ____.

choices.A: determine how concentrated the copper sulfate solution was at the start.
choices.B: increase in this world.
choices.C: stay unchanged.
choices.D: decrease.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal prior knowledge about concentration and product amount.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the panel-to-panel visual pattern showing that precipitate amount changes with concentration.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the direction of explanation by making the precipitate amount control the starting concentration.

reasoning_chain: The panels indicate that more concentrated copper sulfate corresponds to less blue precipitate after the same sodium hydroxide is added. Completing the other side of the same rule, a less concentrated copper sulfate solution must therefore produce more blue precipitate in this world.
required_induction: In this world, decreasing the copper sulfate concentration leads to a larger amount of blue precipitate after equal sodium hydroxide is added.

mechanism_name: Reactant Concentration and Amount of Precipitate Formed
commonsense_rule: Copper sulfate solution concentration up -> amount of blue precipitate formed after adding the same amount of sodium hydroxide up
factual_rule: Copper sulfate solution concentration up -> amount of blue precipitate formed after adding the same amount of sodium hydroxide up

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the CuSO₄ solution concentration were increased to 50% while the same amount of NaOH was added?

choices.A: The amount of blue precipitate would decide how concentrated the CuSO₄ solution becomes.
choices.B: The precipitate would go back up to around the amount seen at 20%.
choices.C: Even less blue precipitate would form than at 35%, possibly only a trace at the bottom.
choices.D: A larger amount of blue precipitate would form.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal chemistry intuition that a higher reactant concentration should produce more precipitate.

partial_error.reason: It invents a reversal point that is not shown in the visual pattern, which consistently decreases as concentration rises.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating precipitate amount as the cause of solution concentration.

reasoning_chain: The panels show that as CuSO₄ concentration increases from 8% to 20% to 35%, the blue precipitate at the bottom gets smaller, going from about half the tube to about one quarter to only a thin layer. This indicates the image's counterfactual rule is that higher CuSO₄ concentration leads to less precipitate when the same amount of NaOH is added. Since 50% is beyond 35% in the same direction, the precipitate should be even less than the thin layer seen at 35%.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the CuSO₄ concentration causes the amount of blue precipitate formed with the same added NaOH to decrease.
